ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for cooked amaranth grain; SNOMED CT coverage for specialty grains is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cooked amaranth grain is approximately 1 cup (246 g), equivalent to a standard side-dish or porridge portion.

nutrient_source.title: Amaranth grain, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170683)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 170683 'Amaranth grain, cooked' is an exact match. HPB Singapore values for energy (102 kcal), protein (3.8 g), fat (1.58 g), carbohydrate (18.69 g), sodium (6 mg), dietary fibre (2.1 g), potassium (135 mg), calcium (47 mg), and iron (2.1 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data and are retained. Additional micronutrient fields are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cooked amaranth grain GI is reported in the range of 35–40 in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008; Brand-Miller et al.). A GI of 35 (low category) is used as a conservative estimate consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database listing for whole cooked amaranth. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (35) × carbohydrate per serving (246 g × 18.69 g / 100 g = 45.98 g) / 100 = 16.1; rounded to 16.1 for 246 g serving. For a standard 100 g reference: GL = 35 × 18.69 / 100 = 6.5. Per 246 g serving GL = 35 × 45.98 / 100 = 16.1. Value recorded as 16.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (6 mg/100 g) with moderate potassium (135 mg/100 g) and magnesium (65 mg/100 g) supporting vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: Cooked amaranth grain is suitable for hypertensive individuals due to its very low sodium content (6 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (135 mg/100 g) and magnesium (65 mg/100 g) levels, which support blood pressure regulation consistent with DASH dietary principles; avoid adding salt during cooking.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35), moderate carbohydrate (18.69 g/100 g) buffered by dietary fibre (2.1 g) and protein (3.8 g) slowing glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cooked amaranth grain has a low glycemic index (~35) and its combination of dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g) and protein (3.8 g/100 g) moderates postprandial glucose response, making it a suitable carbohydrate source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions and paired with non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.34 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g), and magnesium (65 mg/100 g) supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cooked amaranth grain is cardioprotective due to its low saturated fat (0.34 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g) content, which supports LDL reduction; its magnesium (65 mg/100 g) and potassium (135 mg/100 g) further contribute to vascular health and reduced cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Gluten-free whole grain with dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome health relevant to immune regulation; low saturated fat with no pro-inflammatory components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cooked amaranth grain is gluten-free and provides dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g), calcium (47 mg/100 g), and iron (2.1 mg/100 g) with negligible saturated fat, making it suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; combining with omega-3-rich foods such as flaxseed or walnuts further supports an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cooked amaranth grain is a whole-grain pseudocereal prepared by simmering raw amaranth seeds in water until soft and fluffy, typically at a 1:2.5 grain-to-water ratio for approximately 20 minutes. The result is tiny, beige-coloured seeds with a soft, slightly gelatinous texture and a mild, nutty flavour. Amaranth (Amaranthus cruentus or A. hypochondriacus) originates from Mesoamerica and was a staple crop of the Aztec civilisation; it is now consumed globally as a health food and gluten-free grain alternative. Per 100 g cooked, it provides 102 kcal, 3.8 g protein, 1.58 g fat (of which 0.34 g saturated), 18.69 g carbohydrate, 2.1 g dietary fibre, 6 mg sodium, 135 mg potassium, 47 mg calcium, 65 mg magnesium, and 2.1 mg iron. Its low glycemic index (~35) and fibre-protein combination make it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes. Its very low sodium and meaningful potassium and magnesium content align with DASH dietary principles for hypertension. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and plant-based, making it appropriate for a wide range of dietary requirements including those with coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity. Clinically, it is a useful carbohydrate source for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, hypertension, and Type 2 Diabetes.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore and are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170683) for cooked amaranth grain, an exact food match. Micronutrient values are drawn from USDA SR Legacy. GI is supported by peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) and the University of Sydney GI Database. All values are internally consistent and cross-validated between two approved sources.
notes: Sugar_g is reported as 0.0 per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170683); this is a confirmed value for cooked amaranth grain with negligible free sugars. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 as confirmed by USDA SR Legacy; amaranth grain contains no industrially produced trans fats. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as amaranth is a plant food containing no cholesterol. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy for cooked amaranth. Glycemic load per serving (246 g) = GI (35) × carbohydrate per serving (45.98 g) / 100 = 16.1. The glycemic_load_per_serving field is set to 16.1 for the 246 g serving size. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.39 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.69 g), and saturated_fat_g (0.34 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170683. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311069 is the closest defensible match for amaranth grain food product; FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible code for cooked amaranth grain — reviewers should verify FoodEx2 code currency against the EFSA FoodEx2 browser. SNOMED CT does not have a specific code for cooked amaranth grain. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules.
